title: Direct Cinema — Observational Mode
author: Maysles, Wiseman, Pennebaker, Drew (founding figures)
source: 1960s American documentary movement: Robert Drew, the Maysles brothers, Frederick Wiseman, D.A. Pennebaker. Sister tradition: French cinéma vérité (Rouch).

lineage.note: Not a Western tradition geographically (it's American), but methodologically non-Western: rejects imposed narrative structure in favor of observed reality. The editor's role is to find shape in what was filmed, not to impose Snyder beats on it. Often called 'fly-on-the-wall.'

stages.name: Observation
stages.function: What was actually present in the world during shooting. No imposed dramatic arc.

stages.name: Emergence
stages.function: Patterns, repetitions, and turns that the FOOTAGE reveals — not the ones the editor brings.

stages.name: Juxtaposition
stages.function: Meaning produced by adjacency: two scenes next to each other generate understanding the editor didn't pre-script.

stages.name: Rhythm
stages.function: Pacing arises from observed reality — durations of action, natural pauses — not from imposed beat slots.

stages.name: Release
stages.function: Closure comes from a moment that feels final in the world that was filmed, not from a beat-prescribed climax.

cross_framework_equivalence._note: Direct cinema is methodologically opposed to imposed-structure frameworks. The frameworks below describe similar non-imposition philosophies, not equivalent stages.

applicability.limitations: Documentary subjects with strong external structure (a trial, a campaign, a race) resist pure observation because the external structure imposes its own beats whether the editor wants them or not. Direct cinema works best when the SUBJECT itself doesn't have a built-in dramatic arc.
